name: Traditional Goalkeeper
description: The Goalkeeper can distribute the ball in different ways depending on the team's playing strategy.

name: Ball-Playing Goalkeeper
description: The Ball-Playing Goalkeeper looks to play an active role in their team's build-up play, often moving out of their goal to be in a position to recieve the ball.

name: No-Nonsense Goalkeeper
description: The No-Nonsense Goalkeeper adopts a cautious approach, preferring to stay nearer to their goal and taking as few risks as possible with the ball at their feet when put under pressure.

name: Traditional Goalkeeper
description: The Goalkeeper role focuses on protecting their goal and making saves from shots and crosses. They are the last line of defence and are relied upon to help the team keep clean sheets.

name: Sweeper Keeper
description: The Sweeper Keeper is a proactive goalkeeper, regularly looking to come off their line in an attempt to intercept opposition passes or to engage opposition strikers early.

name: Line-Holding Keeper
description: The Line-Holding Keeper is a goalkeeper who primarily looks to stay within the confines of their own box, leaving the job of defending higher up the pitch to their teammates.

name: Centre-Back
description: The main job of the Centre-Back is to provide protection to the team, being in a position where they can be ready to react to attacking threats when the ball is turned over.

name: Advanced Centre-Back
description: The main job of the Centre-Back is to provide protection to the team, being in a position where they can be ready to react to attacking threats when the ball is turned over. It differs from Centre-Back in that the Advanced Centre-Back is generally positioning higher up the field, ready to be involved in build-up play.

name: Ball-Playing Centre-Back
description: The two principle tasks of the Ball-Playing Centre-Back are to provide protection to the team whilst also playing line-breaking passes and through balls from deep to progress the ball up the pitch.

name: No-Nonsense Centre-Back
description: The No-Nonsense Centre-Back is focused on being the last outfield protection a team has, being in a position to be ready to defend at any moment, while also looking to minimize the amount of risks they take with the ball at their feet.

name: Wide Centre-Back
description: Alongside providing defensive protection to the team, the Wide Centre-Back is encouraged to provide width in possession and support to their wide attacking teammates ahead of them, often offering a recycling passing option from deep.

name: Overlapping Centre-Back
description: The Overlapping Centre-Back combines the responsibilities of a Centre-Back and a Winger, looking to provide protection to the team during early phases of attack, while also getting into crossing positions by making overlapping runs in the final third of the pitch.

name: Centre-Back
description: The main job of the Centre-Back is to stop the opposing attackers from playing and to clear the ball from danger when required.

name: Stopping Centre-Back
description: As a Stopper, the Centre-Back is asked to more willing to step out from their position in order to hassle and engage opposition attackers, in an attempt to cut out attacks at their source.

name: Covering Centre-Back
description: As a Cover, the Centre-Back is asked to adopt a more cautious approach, holding their line and waiting to react to the opposition attacker's movements.

name: Wide Centre-Back
description: The main job of the Wide Centre-Back is to stop the opposing attackers from playing and to clear the ball from danger when required.

name: Wide Stopping Centre-Back
description: As a Stopper, the Wide Centre-Back is asked to more willing to step out from their position in order to hassle and engage opposition attackers, in an attempt to cut out attacks at their source.

name: Wide Covering Centre-Back
description: As a Cover, the Wide Centre-Back is asked to adopt a more cautious approach, holding their line and waiting to react to the opposition attacker's movements.

name: Full-Back
description: The Full-Back is a key player in modern football, having to supplement their traditional defensive duties with overlapping runs down the wing to support forward play and help attacks overload the final third.

name: Wing-Back
description: The Wing-Back combines the responsibilities of a Full-Back and a Winger, providing width to the team throughout the attack.

name: Inside Wing-Back
description: Looking to move into central areas of the pitch as their team progresses with the ball, the Inside Wing-Back supports central passing networks, as well as providing vital protection against central counter-attacks when the ball is turned over.

name: Inside Full-Back
description: The Inside Full-Back is a player whose specialist role involves moving inside to become an extra central defender during the attack.

name: Playmaking Wing-Back
description: Similarly to the Inverted Wing-Back, the Playmaking Wing-Back will try to move into central areas of the pitch to support central passing networks and act as a playmaking outlet, whilst offering vital protection against central counter-attacks.

name: Full-Back
description: The main job of a Full-Back is to defend the wide areas as part of the team's backline.

name: Pressing Full-Back
description: As a Presser, the Full-Back is asked to actively engage opposition wide players high up the pitch to support the team's press.

name: Holding Full-Back
description: As a Holder, the Full-Back is asked to hold a deeper position while their team is defending higher up the pitch, in order to provide additional protection against direct attacks.

name: Wing-Back
description: The Wing-Back combines the responsibilities of a Full-Back and a Winger, providing width to the team throughout the attack.

name: Advanced Wing-Back
description: With more aggressive positioning than a traditional Wing-Back, the Advanced Wing-Back looks to play high and wide as much as possible.

name: Inside Wing-Back
description: Looking to move into central areas of the pitch as their team progresses with the ball, the Inside Wing-Back supports central passing networks, as well as providing vital protection against central counter-attacks when the ball is turned over.

name: Playmaking Wing-Back
description: Similarly to the Inverted Wing-Back, the Playmaking Wing-Back will try to move into central areas of the pitch to support central passing networks and act as a playmaking outlet, whilst offering vital protection against central counter-attacks.

name: Wing-Back
description: The Wing-Back is expected to defend the wide areas for their team.

name: Pressing Wing-Back
description: As a Presser, the Wing-Back is asked to aggressively engage opposition wide players high up the pitch to support the team's press.

name: Holding Wing-Back
description: As a Holder, the Wing-Back is asked to hold a deeper position while their team is defending higher up the pitch, in order to provide additional protection against direct attacks.

name: Defensive Midfielder
description: The Defensive Midfielder's main responsibility is to position themselves in front of the team's defence and help protect them from counter-attacking threats.

name: Deep-Lying Playmaker
description: Operating in the space between the defence and the midfield, the Deep-Lying Playmaker aims to initiate attacking moves via pinpoint passes to players positioned higher up the pitch.

name: Box-to-Box Midfielder
description: The non-stop dynamism of the Box-to-Box Midfielder enables them to contribute greatly to both attacking play during the build-up and in the final third.

name: Half-Back
description: A defensive midfielder who looks to drop into their team's defensive line as they progress the ball up the pitch, the Half-Back acts as an additional central defender.

name: Box-to-Box Playmaker
description: The Box-to-Box Playmaker is the heartbeat of their team, looking to be a creative outlet from both deep and advanced areas of the pitch.

name: Defensive Midfielder
description: The Defensive Midfielder's main responsibility out of possession is to position themselves in front of the team's defence and help protect them from attacking threats.

name: Dropping Defensive Midfielder
description: As a Dropper, the defensive midfielder will look to move back into the defensive line as their team are pinned back into their own defensive third, acting as an additional central defender.

name: Pressing Defensive Midfielder
description: As a Presser, the defensive midfielder is tasked with stepping forward from their position to support the team's press high up the pitch.

name: Screening Defensive Midfielder
description: As a Screener, the defensive midfielder's main responsibility is to defend the space in front of their central defenders.

name: Wide Covering Defensive Midfielder
description: As a Wide Cover, the defensive midfielder is asked to provide defensive support in wide areas, moving across from their typical position to help their team defend against wide threats.

name: Central Midfielder
description: Responsible for providing an industrious and versatile link between the defence and the attack, the Central Midfielder is expected to perform a variety of tasks across the centre of the pitch. The role requires the tactical awareness and technical ability to support both defensive and attacking play as needed.

name: Attacking Midfielder
description: An offensive role which looks to operate between the opposition defensive and midfield line, the Attacking Midfielder looks to recieve passes in pockets of space as much as possible.

name: Advanced Playmaker
description: The Advanced Playmaker is a creative role which looks to operate high up the pitch between the opposition's midfield and defence, looking to recieve passes in pockets of space as much as possible.

name: Wide Central Midfielder
description: Typically utilised at the side of a midfield duo or trio, the Wide Central Midfielder's responsibilities lie in providing an industrious and versatile link between the defence and the attack.

name: Channel Midfielder
description: The Channel Midfielder looks to make supporting runs into wide areas, often linking up their team's wide attackers.

name: Midfield Playmaker
description: The Midfield Playmaker operates as a creative link between defence and attack, looking to play progressive passes and killer balls to attacking teammates to initiate attacks for their team.

name: Central Midfielder
description: The Central Midfielder looks to provide protection in the middle of the pitch, often looking to provide a balance between supporting the press and screening passes through the middle.

name: Pressing Central Midfielder
description: As a Presser, the central midfielder is tasked with stepping forward from their position to support the team's press high up the pitch.

name: Screening Central Midfielder
description: As a Screener, the central midfielder's main responsibility is to defend the space in front of their central defenders.

name: Wide Covering Central Midfielder
description: As a Wide Cover, the central midfielder is asked to provide defensive support in wide areas, moving across from their typical position to help their team defend against wide threats.

name: Attacking Midfielder
description: An offensive role which looks to operate between the opposition defensive and midfield line, the Attacking Midfielder looks to recieve passes in pockets of space as much as possible.

name: Advanced Playmaker
description: The Advanced Playmaker is a creative role which looks to operate high up the pitch between the opposition's midfield and defence, looking to recieve passes in pockets of space as much as possible.

name: Free Role
description: A player with a Free Role is given full creative license, allowing them to roam from their position to create attacking opportunities for their team whilst also being encouraged to take more risks with the ball in an effort to both create and score goals.

name: Second Striker
description: The Second Striker operates as one of the team's main goalscoring threats.

name: Channel Midfielder
description: The Channel Midfielder looks to make supporting runs into wide areas, often linking up their team's wide attackers.

name: Attacking Midfielder
description: The Attacking Midfielder looks to defend central areas of the pitch, often being tasked with preventing access to the opposition's midfield pivots.

name: Tracking Attacking Midfielder
description: As a Tracker, the Attacking Midfielder is instructed to track back defensively as the opposition advance in order to support the defenders behind them, dropping back into a deeper defensive position.

name: Central Outlet Attacking Midfielder
description: As a Central Outlet, the Attacking Midfielder is asked to avoid tracking back to help out defenders behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.

name: Splitting Outlet Attacking Midfielder
description: As a Splitting Outlet, the Attacking Midfielder is asked to avoid tracking back to help out defenders behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.

name: Wide Midfielder
description: A wide player who tends to support the attack from deeper than a typical attacking wide player, the Wide Midfielder prefers to play dangerous passes and crosses from deep instead of trying to beat an opposition full-back, trusting their technical ability more than dribbling or pace.

name: Winger
description: Providing width throughout the attack, the Winger stretches the opposition defence by attacking from close to the sidelines. The role aims to provide support to their team's forwards, playing dangerous passes and crosses in the final third to create chances for their teammates.

name: Playmaking Winger
description: The Playmaking Winger will act as the team's primary source of creativity, starting from a wide position during their team's build-up phase before later drifting inside to find space from which to play killer balls and create chances.

name: Inside Winger
description: Frequently aiming to drift inside into the half-space as their team attacks, the Inside Winger looks to recieve the ball in more central areas of the pitch, while also creating space out wide for overlapping teammates. The role is usually combined with attacking full-back or wing-back that looks to hug the sideline so that both players have their own space to operate in.

name: Wide Midfielder
description: The Wide Midfielder provides a balance between defensive protection against wide attacking threats, and being in a position to spring a counter in case of a turnover.

name: Tracking Wide Midfielder
description: As a Tracker, the Wide Midfielder is instructed to track back defensively as the opposition advance, in order to support the defenders behind them, at times even dropping into the backline as an additional defender.

name: Wide Outlet Wide Midfielder
description: As a Wide Outlet, the Wide Midfielder is asked to avoid tracking back to help out the Full-Back behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.

name: Winger
description: Providing width throughout the attack, the Winger stretches the opposition defence by attacking from close to the sidelines. The role aims to provide support to their team's forwards, playing dangerous passes and crosses in the final third to create chances for their teammates.

name: Inside Forward
description: The Inside Forward is a wide role that aims to frequently drift inside into the half-spaces as their team attacks to help open up space for overlapping full-backs, as well as being in position to recieve the ball in more central areas of the pitch.

name: Playmaking Winger
description: The Playmaking Winger will act as the team's primary source of creativity, starting from a wide position during their team's build-up phase before later drifting inside to find space from which to play killer balls and create chances.

name: Wide Forward
description: As with the Winger, the Wide Forward is expected to provide width throughout the attack so as to stretch out the opposition defence and attack from near the sidelines.

name: Inside Winger
description: Frequently aiming to drift inside into the half-space as their team attacks, the Inside Winger looks to recieve the ball in more central areas of the pitch, while also creating space out wide for overlapping teammates. The role is usually combined with attacking full-back or wing-back that looks to hug the sideline so that both players have their own space to operate in.

name: Winger
description: The Winger primarily looks to defend the wide areas of the pitch, looking to find a balance between supporting the press higher up the pitch, and tracking back when necessary to support the wide defender behind them.

name: Tracking Winger
description: As a Tracker, the Winger is instructed to track back defensively as the opposition advance, in order to support the defenders behind them, at times even dropping into the backline as an additional defender.

name: Inside Outlet Winger
description: As a Inside Outlet, the Winger is asked to avoid tracking back to help out the Full-Back behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.

name: Wide Outlet Winger
description: As a Wide Outlet, the Winger is asked to avoid tracking back to help out the Full-Back behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.

name: Deep-Lying Forward
description: With the main function of the Deep-Lying Forward being to link the attack to the midfield, they seek to drop between the lines to recieve passes from deep before supplying linking passes of their own to teammates in a number of ways: moving the ball back to midfield support, spreading it wide to the flanks, or, if they have time to turn, playing in their strike partner.

name: Centre Forward
description: The Centre Forward, also typically known as the classic "Number 9", is expected to lead the line and look to spearhead attacking moves.

name: Target Forward
description: Heavily utilizing their physicality and height to disrupt the opposition's defence and create goalscoring opportunities for their team, the Target Forward is typically the team's attacking outlet during the build-up phase, and are often the target for longer passes and clearances that they knock down, flick on or lay off to their surrounding teammates.

name: Poacher
description: The Poacher sits on the shoulder of the last defender, looking to break the defensive line and run onto through balls from the midfield.

name: Channel Forward
description: A high-energy role, the Channel Forward looks to constantly be a thorn in opposition defender's sides, regularly making supporting runs into the channels for their team.

name: False Nine
description: An unconventional centre forward, the False Nine regularly drops between the lines of the opposition's defence and midfield, looking to act similarly to an Advanced Playmaker for their team.

name: Centre Forward
description: The Centre Forward sets the tone for their team's defensive strategy, often starting the press, or screening passes through the middle of the pitch.

name: Tracking Centre Forward
description: As a Tracker, the Centre Forward is instructed to track back defensively as the opposition advance in order to support the defenders behind them, dropping back into a deeper defensive position.

name: Central Outlet Centre Forward
description: As a Central Outlet, the Centre Forward is asked to avoid tracking back to help out defenders behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.

name: Splitting Outlet Centre Forward
description: As a Splitting Outlet, the Centre Forward is asked to avoid tracking back to help out defenders behind them as much, instead looking to hold their position more in order to be in a dangerous position for a counter-attack when the ball turns over.
